affected_groups: village residents dependent on negotiated outcomes
content.decision_question: Should Hungary construct a state-initiated contractual instrument modeled on French conventions ruralité despite the absence of genuine municipal bargaining power, or reject this transplant as fundamentally mismatched to Hungary's centralized fiscal-legal structure?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence can establish whether municipalities possess or lack fiscal capacity to honor reorganization commitments, and can track whether the state honors funding guarantees over time. It cannot determine whether asymmetric bargaining power itself constitutes an acceptable basis for policy, which is a value question about fairness in negotiation rather than a resolvable empirical matter.
content.tension: T2's reciprocal contracts aim to re-couple municipal commitment with state financing, but Hungary's centralization stripped municipalities of the fiscal capacity and legal standing that make such contracts meaningful in France, so any Hungarian version risks becoming a state-initiated formality that imposes co-responsibility without matching devolved authority or funding-formula relief.
content.title: Fiscal cost transparency versus symbolic versus substantive municipal contracting
value_poles: fiscal realism and avoiding unfunded obligations
central fiscal control versus local accountability
